course.title: Modern Kubernetes 1.36: Workloads, Security, Networking, and Operations
course.description: Developers and operators who understand Linux and Docker and need to design, deploy, secure, debug, package, scale, upgrade, and defend production-shaped Kubernetes applications. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate kubernetes 1.36.2 architecture, api discovery, reconciliation, kubectl context safety, metadata, namespaces, pods, configuration, and observable object-state evidence through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate workload controllers, probes, resources, services, endpointslices, dns, networkpolicy, gateway api 1.5.1, persistent storage, scheduling, disruption, and autoscaling through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate service accounts, least-privilege rbac, secrets, pod security, workload isolation, admission, multi-tenancy, observability, debugging, and incident reconstruction through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate kustomize through kubectl 1.36, helm 4.2.2, extensions, operators, minikube 1.38.1, immutable promotion, conformance, upgrades, fault testing, disaster recovery, and production transfer through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

credential.title: Modern Kubernetes 1.36: Workloads, Security, Networking, and Operations Certificate

modules.title: Kubernetes Purpose, Architecture, Reconciliation, and Environment Evidence
modules.description: A civic platform team must decide which availability problem Kubernetes can solve without mistaking orchestration for application correctness or infrastructure durability. Learners cumulatively produce cluster architecture and reconciliation evidence map through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager, kubelet, container runtime, network proxy, DNS, and add-on responsibilities for one workload transition.
Explain how API requests, admission, persisted desired state, watches, caches, and component status relate without treating any one cache as authority.
Trace desired state, observed state, controller decisions, idempotent actions, status updates, and eventual convergence through a changed failure.

modules.title: API Resources, Object Identity, Schemas, Desired State, and Status
modules.description: A shared cluster rejects and defaults manifests differently across supported API versions while stale status leads a release team toward the wrong repair. Learners cumulatively produce versioned API object contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Resolve API group, version, kind, plural resource, scope, endpoint, and served or stored version for a concrete object.
Separate desired spec, controller-owned status, metadata generation, observedGeneration, and status conditions before judging convergence.
Use namespace, name, UID, resourceVersion, generation, timestamps, and managed fields to distinguish identity, concurrency, and history.

modules.title: kubectl Discovery, Contexts, Queries, Diff, Apply, and Safe Mutation
modules.description: An operator has two similarly named clusters and a manifest owned by multiple field managers; a convenient imperative edit could hit the wrong authority and steal fields. Learners cumulatively produce safe kubectl investigation and apply record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace kubeconfig loading, merged files, context, cluster, user, namespace, credentials, impersonation, and TLS server identity before a request.
Use API discovery, api-resources, api-versions, explain, raw endpoints, and versioned reference material to identify authoritative fields.
Choose get, describe, events, logs, JSONPath, custom columns, selectors, and raw JSON or YAML without parsing human tables as stable interfaces.

modules.title: Namespaces, Metadata, Labels, Selectors, Ownership, and Deletion
modules.description: Several teams share a cluster where a selector overlaps another release, an owner is recreated, and a finalizer leaves a namespace terminating. Learners cumulatively produce resource identity and lifecycle policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish namespaced and cluster-scoped resources, default namespaces, cross-namespace references, quotas, and isolation limits.
Design stable recommended labels and equality or set selectors without accidental overlap, mutable identity, or controller mismatch.
Separate identifying labels from non-identifying annotations and preserve controller, tooling, audit, and rollout metadata ownership.

modules.title: Pods, Multi-Container Composition, Lifecycle, Termination, and Debug Containers
modules.description: An accessible status service shares a Pod with initialization and support containers but loses requests during termination and hides its first crash. Learners cumulatively produce bounded Pod composition and lifecycle manifest through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Explain Pod identity, scheduling unit, shared network and volumes, isolated process and filesystem boundaries, and ephemeral replacement.
Choose init, app, native sidecar, adapter, ambassador, and helper containers from startup, restart, ownership, shutdown, and resource contracts.
Trace waiting, running, terminated, reasons, exit codes, restartPolicy, backoff, previous state, and Pod phase without equating them.

modules.title: ConfigMaps, Secrets, Environment, Projection, Rotation, and Change Propagation
modules.description: A payment-free intake service mixes public config with credentials, assumes environment values rotate, and releases without proving how changed data reaches running Pods. Learners cumulatively produce configuration and secret propagation contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Create and consume ConfigMaps through keys, files, env, envFrom, command arguments, immutable objects, and size or naming boundaries.
Distinguish base64 representation, API and etcd encryption, transport protection, RBAC, node exposure, application use, and external secret authority.
Compose Secret, ConfigMap, service account token, downward API, and cluster trust bundle projections with scoped paths, modes, audiences, and refresh behavior.

modules.title: Requests, Limits, QoS, Probes, Startup, Readiness, and Resource Failure
modules.description: A slow-starting API is killed by liveness checks, serves traffic before warmup, and competes for memory without meaningful requests or limits. Learners cumulatively produce health and resource behavior policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Relate CPU and memory requests to scheduling and accounting, limits to runtime enforcement, and application concurrency to measured capacity.
Derive Guaranteed, Burstable, and BestEffort QoS, container OOM, Pod eviction, node pressure, and restart evidence from effective resources.
Separate startup gating, traffic readiness, process liveness, container restart, endpoint conditions, and dependency health.

modules.title: Deployments, ReplicaSets, Rollouts, Strategies, Conditions, and Recovery
modules.description: A release changes image and config while progress stalls, old ReplicaSets remain, and a rollback would restore code without restoring external state. Learners cumulatively produce observable Deployment rollout and recovery plan through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace Deployment template hash, ReplicaSet ownership, desired replicas, Pod replacement, adoption boundaries, and observed status.
Calculate RollingUpdate maxSurge, maxUnavailable, total capacity, readiness, termination, and rollout duration under a changed replica count.
Bind Pod template changes to immutable image digests, configuration identity, pull policy, provenance, and selected platform evidence.

modules.title: StatefulSets, DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs, and Controller Selection
modules.description: A data intake system has interchangeable APIs, identity-bound workers, node agents, finite migrations, and scheduled reconciliation tasks requiring different controllers. Learners cumulatively produce controller selection and completion contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Design StatefulSet ordinal identity, headless discovery, stable claims, ordering, update partition, scaling, and failure recovery around application guarantees.
Use DaemonSets for node-local facilities with selectors, tolerations, priority, rolling updates, resource bounds, and control-plane coverage decisions.
Model Job completions, parallelism, indexed work, retries, backoff, deadlines, success policy, duplicate execution, and cleanup.

modules.title: Services, EndpointSlices, DNS, Discovery, and Traffic Policies
modules.description: A frontend loses backends as Pods rotate because it pins Pod IPs; a Service selector overlaps a canary and traffic policies change source and locality behavior. Learners cumulatively produce service discovery and endpoint evidence map through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Explain Pod IP identity, node routing, ports, host network exceptions, replacement, dual-stack boundaries, and application bind behavior.
Trace Service selectors, EndpointSlices, readiness and serving conditions, manual endpoints, topology hints, and stale endpoint diagnosis.
Choose ClusterIP, headless, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName, or selectorless Service from discovery, exposure, platform, and security constraints.

modules.title: Ingress, Gateway API 1.5.1, Routing, TLS, Delegation, and Status
modules.description: Platform and application teams must share external HTTP routing without annotation-specific coupling, cross-namespace privilege, stale status, or unverified TLS ownership. Learners cumulatively produce Gateway API routing and delegation policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Compare Service, Ingress, implementation-specific annotations, Gateway API, controller installation, data plane, and conformance boundaries.
Select GatewayClass and implementation from controller identity, parameters, supported features, conformance reports, release channel, and operational ownership.
Design Gateway addresses and listeners with protocol, port, hostname, namespace attachment, certificate reference, TLS mode, and conflict behavior.

modules.title: Cluster Networking, CNI, NetworkPolicy, Dual Stack, and Layered Troubleshooting
modules.description: A multi-tier application has working DNS but failed connections, an additive policy gap, asymmetric dual-stack behavior, and a provider-specific data plane. Learners cumulatively produce network intent and isolation evidence policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace Pod addressing, routing, CNI responsibilities, Service implementation, node paths, NAT, MTU, and platform-specific exceptions.
Interpret single or dual-stack families, family policy, ClusterIPs, EndpointSlices, service proxies, source addresses, and asymmetric reachability.
Derive ingress and egress isolation from Pod and namespace selectors, ipBlock, ports, peers, policy types, and additive allow rules.

modules.title: Volumes, PersistentVolumes, Claims, StorageClasses, CSI, Snapshots, and Recovery
modules.description: A stateful intake service binds storage in the wrong zone, deletes data through a reclaim policy, and treats a crash-consistent snapshot as a proven application restore. Learners cumulatively produce stateful storage and recovery contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish image, writable layer, emptyDir, projected, hostPath, ephemeral CSI, persistent, and external application storage lifecycles and authority.
Trace PV and PVC capacity, access modes, volume mode, selectors, binding, claim references, node affinity, phases, expansion, and release.
Design StorageClasses with provisioner, parameters, defaulting, reclaim policy, expansion, mount options, and WaitForFirstConsumer topology behavior.

modules.title: Scheduling, Affinity, Taints, Topology, Priority, Preemption, and Eviction
modules.description: A critical workload remains Pending despite free aggregate capacity and later concentrates replicas into one failure domain while lower-priority work is disrupted. Learners cumulatively produce placement and disruption decision record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use trusted node labels, nodeSelector, required and preferred node affinity, operators, weights, and ignored-during-execution semantics.
Design inter-Pod affinity and anti-affinity with selectors, namespaces, topology keys, scheduler cost, and failure-domain consequences.
Trace NoSchedule, PreferNoSchedule, and NoExecute taints, matching tolerations, toleration time, node conditions, and admission-added behavior.

modules.title: Availability, Disruption Budgets, Graceful Draining, Autoscaling, and Capacity
modules.description: A two-zone service scales from noisy metrics, violates its recovery target during drain, and has enough replicas but insufficient failure-domain and resource headroom. Learners cumulatively produce availability and autoscaling operating policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Translate service objectives into replica count, failure domains, topology placement, readiness, surge capacity, dependency limits, and recovery evidence.
Calculate minAvailable or maxUnavailable, unhealthy eviction policy, controller scale, simultaneous drains, and voluntary versus involuntary disruption limits.
Derive HPA desired replicas from requests, utilization, custom or external metrics, missing samples, tolerance, stabilization, and scale policies.

modules.title: Authentication, Authorization, RBAC, Service Accounts, Tokens, and Impersonation
modules.description: A CI workload uses the default service account and a long-lived token while a broad ClusterRoleBinding permits secret reads and workload creation across tenants. Learners cumulatively produce least-privilege identity and authorization policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace TLS identity, authentication, user and group information, authorization, admission, validation, persistence, and audit for one request.
Separate external human users and groups, ServiceAccounts, node identities, workload identity federation, image pull identity, and bootstrap credentials.
Construct namespace-scoped Roles and RoleBindings or justified ClusterRoles and ClusterRoleBindings from verbs, resources, names, non-resource URLs, and subjects.

modules.title: Pod Security, Security Contexts, Images, Admission, Secrets, and Multi-Tenancy
modules.description: A hostile document workload requests privileged host access, mutable images, broad credentials, unrestricted egress, and co-location with sensitive tenants. Learners cumulatively produce restricted workload and admission policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Apply Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted Pod Security Standards with enforce, audit, warn, version pinning, exemptions, namespace labels, and rollout evidence.
Set non-root identity, groups, filesystem ownership, read-only root, capabilities, privilege escalation, seccomp, host namespaces, devices, and writable mounts from threat evidence.
Require immutable images, provenance, signatures, vulnerability policy, registry allowlists, architecture, pull credentials, and admission decisions with exceptions.

modules.title: Status, Events, Logs, Metrics, Traces, Audit, and Layered Incident Response
modules.description: A release has green desired replicas but user failures, overwritten events, restarted containers, missing cluster logs, high-cardinality metrics, and no cross-layer correlation. Learners cumulatively produce cluster and workload incident evidence record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Correlate spec, observedGeneration, conditions, reasons, messages, events, resource versions, controller ownership, and event retention without treating event order as durable history.
Collect current and previous container logs, multi-container streams, timestamps, kubelet and runtime logs, rotation, retention, and node-loss boundaries.
Distinguish resource metrics, component metrics, application metrics, state metrics, cardinality, scrape, storage, alert, and autoscaling evidence.

modules.title: Kustomize, Helm 4, Rendering, Validation, Field Ownership, and Promotion
modules.description: A platform team copies environment manifests, renders an unreviewed chart, stores credentials in values, and promotes mutable images without diff, ownership, or rollback evidence. Learners cumulatively produce rendered environment release package through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Compose Kustomize resources, components, overlays, prefixes, namespaces, labels, images, replacements, and patches without corrupting selectors or source bases.
Generate ConfigMaps and Secrets from external sources, reason about name hashes and references, restrict loading, and keep secret values out of rendered evidence.
Inspect Helm 4 charts, templates, values schema, dependencies, hooks, release state, OCI identity, install, upgrade, rollback, uninstall, and ownership boundaries.

modules.title: CRDs, Controllers, Operators, Admission Policies, Webhooks, and Extension Trust
modules.description: A cluster extension stores an underspecified custom resource, mutates workloads through an unavailable webhook, and upgrades without conversion or rollback evidence. Learners cumulatively produce trusted extension API and controller contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Design CRD names, scope, versions, structural OpenAPI schemas, defaulting, validation, pruning, status subresource, scale, storage version, and conversion.
Build or evaluate controllers from watches, work queues, level-based reconciliation, ownership, idempotency, finalizers, status, retries, leader election, and rate limits.
Choose built-in admission, ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, validating webhook, or mutating webhook from expressiveness, failure policy, ordering, timeout, availability, and audit needs.

modules.title: Local Clusters, Cluster Lifecycle, Conformance, Upgrades, Backup, and Production Defense
modules.description: An operations board must distinguish a local minikube proof from production, upgrade a supported cluster without skipped minors, recover control-plane state, and defend remaining risks. Learners cumulatively produce cluster lifecycle and recovery defense through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Create and inspect a minikube 1.38.1 learning cluster while naming driver, node, runtime, networking, storage, add-on, tunnel, resource, and production-transfer limits.
Trace kubeadm bootstrap, certificates, static Pods, control-plane endpoints, etcd membership, kubelet configuration, CNI installation, join tokens, and high-availability responsibilities.
Evaluate Kubernetes conformance, supported versions, distribution lifecycle, cloud integration, add-ons, feature gates, APIs, operational ownership, and vendor support.
